Understanding distinction between RxJS and Signals is one of the most important architectural decisions in modern Angular.
Angular 16 introduced Signals and it immediately spark controversy. Many assumed that it will replace RxJS or RxJS will become obsolete and we have to rewrite all the functionalities using Signals. This is because RxJS was used heavily. (remember AngularJS 1 to Angular?)
However, in reality, Signals solves different problem and is not a replacement of RxJS. Let’s discuss in this article.
What are they — brief introduction
RxJS (Reactive Extensions for JavaScript) is a library for working with asynchronous data streams. A stream represents values that arrive over time. These values might come from an HTTP request, user interactions, WebSocket messages, timers, or any other asynchronous source.
Imagine an e-commerce application where a customer places an order. The order status changes over time from “Order Placed” to “Processing,” then “Shipped,” and finally “Delivered.” Each status update is an event that occurs at different moments. RxJS is designed to model and process these kinds of event streams.
A simplified example might look like this:
orderStatus$ = this.orderService.getOrderStatus(orderId);
orderStatus$.subscribe(status => { console.log(status); });Here, we may get multiple values over time as the order progresses through different stages.
Signals, on the other hand, are Angular’s reactive functionality for managing state. A Signal always represents the current value of something. Instead of describing a sequence of events over time, a Signal describes the latest state.
Using the same e-commerce example, suppose a user selects a product from a product list. The currently selected product is not a stream of events. It is simply the current state of the UI. Signals are ideal for this scenario.
selectedProduct = signal<Product | null>(null);
selectProduct(product: Product) {
this.selectedProduct.set(product);
}At any moment, the signal holds the current selected product. Components can read it synchronously and Angular automatically updates the UI whenever the value changes.
This leads to an important distinction.
RxJS answers the question: “What values might arrive over time?”
Signals answer the question: “What is the current value right now?”
That single difference explains most of the architectural decisions we make when choosing between them.
The Evolution of Reactivity in Angular
In the early days, Angular applications relied heavily on change detection that traversed large portions of the component tree whenever something changed. As applications grew larger, developers increasingly adopted RxJS to manage asynchronous operations and application state.
RxJS became the foundation of many Angular applications. Developers used Observables for HTTP requests, component communication, state management, user events, and nearly every reactive scenario imaginable.
While RxJS is extremely powerful, it also comes with complexity. Developers must understand operators such as switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, combineLatest, debounceTime, and many others. While these operators provide tremendous flexibility, they can also make code difficult to understand and maintain when used for simple state management scenarios.
Signals were introduced to simplify reactive state management inside Angular applications. They provide a more intuitive way to represent state while allowing Angular to perform more efficient updates.
Signals were not created to replace RxJS. They were created to eliminate unnecessary RxJS usage where state management was the primary goal.
Why Signals Feel Faster
One reason Signals have generated so much excitement is their performance characteristics.
Traditional Angular applications often rely on Observables combined with the async pipe. When data changes, Angular performs change detection to determine what needs to be updated.
Signals take a more granular approach.
When a template reads a signal, Angular automatically tracks that dependency. If the signal changes later, Angular knows exactly which parts of the UI depend on that value.
Instead of checking large portions of the component tree, Angular can update only the affected areas.
For example:
userName = signal('John');
changeName() {
this.userName.set('David');
}If only one section of the template uses userName, Angular updates only that section.
The Biggest Mistake Teams Are Making
Many teams hear that Signals are the future and immediately begin wrapping everything in signals.
Consider this pattern:
products$ = this.http.get<Product[]>('/api/products');
products = toSignal(products$);Technically, this works. However, many teams stop here and assume they have adopted Signal-based architecture.
In reality, they have simply converted an Observable into a Signal and the underlying architecture remains unchanged.
Signals should not be viewed as a decorative layer placed on top of existing RxJS code. Instead, developers should identify which parts of their applications represent state and which parts represent streams.
Only then can Signals be used effectively.
When Signals Are the Better Choice
Signals excel at representing UI state.
Consider a product details page. The application may need to track whether data is loading, which product is selected, which tab is active, and whether a modal dialog is open.
These values all represent current state.
loading = signal(false);
selectedProduct = signal<Product | null>(null);
activeTab = signal('details');
isReviewDialogOpen = signal(false);Signals make this code straightforward and easy to understand. They also shine when creating derived state through computed signals. Suppose an e-commerce application displays only products currently in stock.
products = signal<Product[]>([]);
availableProducts = computed(() =>
this.products().filter(p => p.inStock)
);The computed signal automatically updates whenever the products list changes.
The resulting code is concise, readable, and requires minimal boilerplate.
When RxJS Remains the Right Tool
Despite the excitement around Signals, RxJS remains indispensable.
HTTP requests are naturally asynchronous streams.
this.http.get<Product[]>('/api/products');WebSocket communication is a stream.
Server-Sent Events are streams.
User interactions such as typing, scrolling, and clicking generate streams of events.
Search functionality provides a perfect example.
searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
debounceTime(300),
distinctUntilChanged(),
switchMap(query =>
this.productService.search(query)
)
);Here, RxJS provides powerful operators that allow developers to debounce user input, cancel previous requests, and process asynchronous responses efficiently.
Signals cannot replace these capabilities.
Whenever the problem involves events occurring over time, RxJS remains the superior solution.
The Enterprise Sweet Spot: A Hybrid Architecture
In my experience, the best architecture for modern Angular applications combines both technologies.
The service layer should continue using RxJS because APIs, WebSockets, and asynchronous workflows naturally produce streams.
@Injectable()
export class ProductService {
getProducts() {
return this.http.get<Product[]>('/api/products');
}
}The component layer should primarily use Signals because components are concerned with rendering state.
products = toSignal(
this.productService.getProducts(),
{ initialValue: [] }
);This creates a clean separation of responsibilities. RxJS handles asynchronous workflows. Signals handle UI state.
The resulting architecture is easier to understand, easier to maintain, and aligns closely with Angular’s future direction.
Signals and OnPush: A Powerful Combination
For years, Angular performance optimization often revolved around OnPush change detection.
@Component({
changeDetection:
ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
})Signals complement this strategy beautifully.
Because Angular can track exactly which signals are used within a template, updates become highly targeted and efficient.
Combining Signals with OnPush creates a performance baseline that would previously require significantly more effort to achieve.
Large applications benefit from fewer unnecessary renders, improved responsiveness, and reduced change detection overhead.
Should Signals Replace NgRx?
This is another question that frequently appears in architectural discussions.
For smaller applications, Signals can often eliminate the need for NgRx entirely. Simple state management becomes much easier using signals, computed values, and effects.
However, enterprise applications frequently require capabilities beyond state storage.
Features such as time-travel debugging, action logging, event sourcing, audit trails, and predictable state transitions remain valuable. In these scenarios, NgRx still offers significant benefits.
The future is more likely “NgRx powered by Signals.”
Final Thoughts
The debate between Signals and RxJS is based on a false assumption. The question is not which one should win.
Signals and RxJS are complementary technologies.
RxJS is a general-purpose reactive programming library capable of managing state, events, and asynchronous data streams. Angular Signals focus specifically on application and component state, providing a simpler and more efficient mechanism for UI reactivity inside Angular.
